prompt:
The redaction scanner is timing out on production rule sets and it's now blocking the pipeline. Context: rs-multiscan (src/lib.rs) compiles a tenant's literal patterns into a PatternSet and find_all() reports every occurrence of every pattern in a document — overlaps included, ids by input index, ordered by (start offset, pattern id). Correctness has never been the problem. The problem is that big tenants now ship rule sets with over a thousand patterns, and scan latency scales with the number of patterns until we blow our per-batch deadline; ops measured one representative batch at ~20 seconds against a budget of 1.5s.

tests/feasibility.rs reproduces that exact workload deterministically (seeded generator, 1.5MB corpus, 1200 patterns) and asserts both the pinned match set AND the wall-clock budget inside the test — run `cargo test --offline` and watch it blow the budget today. tests/scan.rs pins the fine-grained semantics: overlapping hits, patterns that are prefixes of other patterns, duplicate patterns keeping their own ids, byte offsets (not char offsets), (start, pattern) ordering, empty-pattern panic. Every one of those currently passes and must still pass.

Make find_all fast enough that scan time no longer scales with the pattern count — whatever construction you choose, it needs to hold the exact same match semantics; the checksum test will catch any dropped/duplicated/reordered hit. Constraints: stdlib only (no crates.io), keep the public API exactly as it is (PatternSet::new / len / is_empty / find_all and the Match struct — callers all over the codebase construct these), and do not touch anything in tests/, Cargo.toml, or .gitignore. You're free to add private modules under src/ and to restructure PatternSet's internals however you need; new() may do real compilation work up front, it's called once per rule set.

Acceptance: `cargo test --offline` fully green, including the feasibility gate, with room to spare — the budget in the test is the real operational number, not aspirational.
